source.sourcePath: raw/sources/RA/04_제품별 인허가 진행 문서/01_HAD1717MC(MG)/▶ CE MDD/■ 3. 3차 CE TCF 보안사항 제출 (12월10일)/05. Software_Validation/Software 62304 checklist_Ver 2019101.xlsx

sourceSummary.oneLine: Checklist mapping IEC 62304 medical device software lifecycle, risk management, configuration management, maintenance, and problem resolution requirements to supporting documents.
sourceSummary.officialUse: Used as software validation or technical file checklist evidence for medical device software lifecycle compliance.
keyPoints: The checklist covers IEC 62304 general requirements, software development, maintenance, risk management, configuration management, and problem resolution processes.
Risk management process compliance with ISO 14971 is identified as applicable for software Classes A, B, and C.
Software safety classes A, B, and C are assigned based on possible patient, operator, or other-person harm severity.

Supporting document A-TCF-5-010 is repeatedly referenced for software development planning, requirements, architecture, verification, validation, testing, maintenance, configuration management, and revision history.
Several SOUP-related requirements are marked Not applicable.

entities.name: ISO 14971
entities.evidence: The MANUFACTURER shall apply a RISK MANAGEMENT PROCESS complying with ISO 14971.

entities.name: CE MDD
entities.evidence: sourcePath: raw/sources/RA/04_제품별 인허가 진행 문서/01_HAD1717MC(MG)/▶ CE MDD/■ 3. 3차 CE TCF 보안사항 제출 (12월10일)/05. Software_Validation/Software 62304 checklist_Ver 2019101.xlsx

concepts.name: Quality management system
concepts.pageTarget: Medical Device Software Quality Management System
concepts.evidence: The MANUFACTURER of MEDICAL DEVICE SOFTWARE shall demonstrate the ability to provide MEDICAL DEVICE SOFTWARE that consistently meets customer requirements and applicable regulatory requirements.

concepts.name: Risk management process
concepts.evidence: The MANUFACTURER shall apply a RISK MANAGEMENT PROCESS complying with ISO 14971.

concepts.name: Software safety classification
concepts.evidence: The MANUFACTURER shall assign to each SOFTWARE SYSTEM a software safety class (A, B or C) according to the possible effects on the patient, operator or other people resulting from a HAZARD to which the SOFTWARE SYSTEM can contribute.

concepts.name: Software development planning
concepts.evidence: The software development lifecycle model is fully defined or referenced by the plan.

concepts.name: Software requirements analysis
concepts.evidence: For each SOFTWARE SYSTEM of the MEDICAL DEVICE, the MANUFACTURER has defined and documented SOFTWARE SYSTEM requirements from the SYSTEM level requirements.

concepts.name: Software architecture design
concepts.evidence: The MANUFACTURER has transformed the requirements for the MEDICAL DEVICE SOFTWARE into a documented ARCHITECTURE that describes the software's structure and identifies the SOFTWARE ITEMS.

concepts.name: Software verification and validation testing
concepts.pageTarget: Medical Device Software Verification and Validation

concepts.name: Software maintenance process
concepts.evidence: The MANUFACTURER has established a software maintenance plan (or plans) for conducting the ACTIVITIES and TASKS of the maintenance PROCESS.

concepts.name: Software configuration management
concepts.evidence: The MANUFACTURER has established a scheme for the unique identification of CONFIGURATION ITEMS and their VERSIONS to be controlled for the project.

concepts.name: Software problem resolution process
concepts.evidence: The MANUFACTURER has prepared a PROBLEM REPORT for each problem detected in a SOFTWARE PRODUCT. PROBLEM REPORTS shall be classified by type, scope and criticality.

concepts.name: SOUP
concepts.evidence: software configuration and change management, including SOUP CONFIGURATION ITEMS and software used to support developmen

evidence.claim: The document is titled Common Sense Systems IEC 62304 Checklist.
evidence.evidenceText: Common Sense Systems IEC 62304 Checklist

evidence.claim: The checklist addresses IEC 62304 medical device software lifecycle processes.
evidence.evidenceText: Reference,Software Lifecycle Process

evidence.claim: The risk management process is required to comply with ISO 14971.
evidence.evidenceText: The MANUFACTURER shall apply a RISK MANAGEMENT PROCESS complying with ISO 14971.

evidence.claim: Software safety classes are A, B, and C and are assigned according to possible harm effects.
evidence.evidenceText: The MANUFACTURER shall assign to each SOFTWARE SYSTEM a software safety class (A, B or C) according to the possible effects on the patient, operator or other people resulting from a HAZARD to which the SOFTWARE SYSTEM can contribute.

evidence.claim: Class A means no injury or damage to health is possible.
evidence.evidenceText: Class A: No injury or damage to health is possible

evidence.claim: Class B means non-serious injury is possible.
evidence.evidenceText: Class B: Non-SERIOUS INJURY is possible

evidence.claim: Class C means death or serious injury is possible.
evidence.evidenceText: Class C: Death or SERIOUS INJURY is possible

evidence.claim: The software development lifecycle model is defined or referenced by the plan.
evidence.evidenceText: The software development lifecycle model is fully defined or referenced by the plan.

evidence.claim: Software requirements are defined and documented from system-level requirements.
evidence.evidenceText: For each SOFTWARE SYSTEM of the MEDICAL DEVICE, the MANUFACTURER has defined and documented SOFTWARE SYSTEM requirements from the SYSTEM level requirements.

evidence.claim: Software requirements include regulatory requirements as appropriate.
evidence.evidenceText: As appropriate to the MEDICAL DEVICE SOFTWARE, the MANUFACTURER has included regulatory requirements in the software requirements.

evidence.claim: Software architecture describes the software structure and identifies software items.
evidence.evidenceText: The MANUFACTURER has transformed the requirements for the MEDICAL DEVICE SOFTWARE into a documented ARCHITECTURE that describes the software's structure and identifies the SOFTWARE ITEMS.

evidence.claim: Software integration testing includes testing integrated software items and documenting results.
evidence.evidenceText: The MANUFACTURER shall test the integrated SOFTWARE ITEMS in accordance with the integration plan (see 5.1.5) and document the results.

evidence.claim: Software system testing covers all software requirements.
evidence.evidenceText: The MANUFACTURER has established and performed a set of tests, expressed as input stimuli, expected outcomes, pass/fail criteria and procedures, for conducting SOFTWARE SYSTEM testing, such that all software requirements have been covered.

evidence.claim: Before release, software verification is completed and evaluated.
evidence.evidenceText: The MANUFACTURER has ensured that software VERIFICATION was completed and the results EVALUATED before the software was released.

evidence.claim: Known residual anomalies are documented and evaluated for unacceptable risk.
evidence.evidenceText: The MANUFACTURER has ensured that all known residual ANOMALIES were EVALUATED to ensure that they did not contribute to an unacceptable risk.

evidence.claim: The software maintenance plan covers feedback after release.
evidence.evidenceText: The software maintenance plan addresses procedures for receiving, documenting, evaluating, resolving and tracking feedback arising after release of the MEDICAL DEVICE SOFTWARE.

evidence.claim: Users and regulators are informed about released software problems as required by local regulation.
evidence.evidenceText: As required by local regulation, the MANUFACTURER has informed users and regulators about any problem in released SOFTWARE PRODUCTS and the consequences of continued unchanged use.

evidence.claim: Software risk management identifies software items that could contribute to hazardous situations identified in ISO 14971 medical device risk analysis.
evidence.evidenceText: The MANUFACTURER has identified SOFTWARE ITEMS that could contribute to a hazardous situation identified in the MEDICAL DEVICE RISK ANALYSIS ACTIVITY of ISO 14971 (see 4.2).

evidence.claim: Risk control measures are defined and documented for each potential software contribution to a hazardous situation.
evidence.evidenceText: For each potential cause of the SOFTWARE ITEM contributing to a hazardous situation documented in the RISK MANAGEMENT FILE, the MANUFACTURER has defined and documented RISK CONTROL measures.

evidence.claim: Software hazard traceability is documented from hazardous situation through software item, cause, risk control measure, and verification.
evidence.evidenceText: The MANUFACTURER has documented TRACEABILITY of software HAZARDS as appropriate, from the RISK CONTROL measure to the VERIFICATION of the RISK CONTROL measure.

evidence.claim: Configuration items and versions are uniquely identified for the project.
evidence.evidenceText: The MANUFACTURER has established a scheme for the unique identification of CONFIGURATION ITEMS and their VERSIONS to be controlled for the project.

evidence.claim: Configuration item changes are made only in response to approved change requests.
evidence.evidenceText: The MANUFACTURER has changed CONFIGURATION ITEMS only in response to approved CHANGE REQUESTs.

evidence.claim: Problem reports are prepared for each detected software product problem and classified by type, scope, and criticality.
evidence.evidenceText: The MANUFACTURER has prepared a PROBLEM REPORT for each problem detected in a SOFTWARE PRODUCT. PROBLEM REPORTS shall be classified by type, scope and criticality.

evidence.claim: Testing after a software change includes software version, configurations, tools, date tested, and tester identification in test documentation.
evidence.evidenceText: When testing, retesting, or REGRESSION TESTING SOFTWARE ITEMS and SYSTEMS following a change, the MANUFACTURER has included the VERSION of software tested in the test documentation.

evidence.claim: SOUP-related requirements are often marked Not applicable in this checklist.
evidence.evidenceText: If a SOFTWARE ITEM is identified as SOUP, the MANUFACTURER has specified functional and performance requirements for the SOUP item that are necessary for its intended use.,,X,X,,Not applicable,,

reviewFlags.reason: Document date is not explicitly present; Ver 2019101 is preserved as revision/version rather than inferred as a date.
Authority is ambiguous: metadata authoritySignal is FDA, while sourcePath indicates CE MDD, and the checklist text itself primarily references IEC 62304 and ISO 14971.
Product model is taken from provided productSignal metadata because the checklist text does not explicitly name a product model.
Source appears to be preprocessed spreadsheet text with duplicated title row and some typographical/OCR-like artifacts such as PROCESESS and developmen.
